context.description: A philosophical essay page contrasts the scientist and the psychologist as living in different symbolic worlds shaped by different gods and monsters. It argues that personality development and changes in what a person wants arise from these underlying symbolic frameworks. The section heading is "Von Wollen und Können".
normalization_notes: German prose page from a nonfiction book.
Section heading translated to English in description and retained in source-language title field.

rows.paragraph_text: A scientist may live like Athena, devoted to knowledge and competing with colleagues for the Nobel Prize, while a psychologist may live like Demeter, seeking to send clients back into the world renewed and bearing the pain of loss with greater empathy.
rows.text_content: The scientist and psychologist embody different symbolic worlds and different guiding figures.

rows.paragraph_text: People filter perception and assign meaning through these symbolic worlds, and personality development often occurs without conscious awareness even though others can clearly notice the change. A person's outer expression changes when the underlying foundation of action changes.
rows.text_content: Personality change is presented as an often-unnoticed shift in the symbolic foundation of action.

rows.paragraph_text: The author introduces the saying that one can do what one wants but cannot want what one wants, then qualifies it by arguing that changes in wanting arise from changes in the symbolic world.
rows.text_content: The section begins an argument about will, wanting, and the symbolic basis of desire.

content.free_text: book_page nonfiction_philosophy symbolism_and_archetypes German nonfiction philosophy page on symbolic worlds, archetypal self-understanding, personality development, and the relation between will and wanting.
